content.problemStatement:
The integers 000 to M−1M - 1M−1 have been arranged in a circular fashion. That is, 0,1,2,…,M−10, 1, 2, \ldots, M - 10,1,2,…,M−1, are in that order and
also, 000 and M−1M - 1M−1 are next to each other. The distance between any two adjacent numbers on this circle is 1. You are given NNN intervals on this, such that no two intervals touch or intersect with each other. The i-th interval will be of the form [Li,RiL_i, R_iLi​,Ri​]. This means that the i-th interval contains all the integers between LiL_iLi​ and RiR_iRi​, both end points inclusive. You are supposed to mark exactly one number inside each interval, in such a way that the minimum distance between any two marked numbers is maximized.

More formally, we have 0≤L1≤R1<L2≤R2<L3…<LN≤RN≤M−10 \leq L_1 \leq R_1 < L_2 \leq R_2 < L_3 \ldots < L_N \leq R_N \leq M - 10≤L1​≤R1​<L2​≤R2​<L3​…<LN​≤RN​≤M−1. You are supposed to mark exactly NNN numbers: A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​, such that Li≤Ai≤RiL_i \leq A_i \leq R_iLi​≤Ai​≤Ri​ for all 1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N. And you want to do it in such a manner mini≠jmin_{i \neq j}mini=j​ (shortest distance between AiA_iAi​ and AjA_jAj​ ), is maximized.

Input:
First line of the input contains a pair of integers MMM and NNN.
The i-th of the next NNN lines contains two numbers LiL_iLi​ and RiR_iRi​ which denote the end points of the i-th interval.
Output:
A single integer denoting the maximized minimum distance between any two marked numbers.
constraints: Li < Lj and Ri < Rj for all i < j
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains two integers M and N. Next N lines each contain two integers Li and Ri describing the i-th interval. Intervals are given in increasing order and do not intersect.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the maximum possible value of the minimum distance between any two marked numbers.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Choosing points 0,3,6 gives minimum circular distance 3, which is optimal.
Choosing points 0 and 4 yields distance 4, the maximum possible.
Points 0,50,100,150,200 give minimum distance 50, which cannot be improved.

solution.approach: The answer is the largest distance d such that we can place one point in each interval with pairwise circular distance at least d. For a fixed d we check feasibility greedily: place the first point as far left as possible, then each next point as far left as possible but at least d away from the previous one (clamping to the interval). After the forward pass we obtain the position of the last point. To satisfy the circular condition we perform a backward pass that pushes earlier points leftwards as much as possible while keeping distance d, finally checking the wrap‑around gap. Feasibility is monotone in d, therefore we binary search d in [0, M]. The check runs in O(N) time, binary search adds a log factor, giving O(N log M) per test case, which easily fits the limits.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log M) per test case
